metadata.summary: Identify and document system boundaries: data elements, protocols, frequency, volume, security. At scale: portfolio landscape register with criticality tiers, data sensitivity, ownership.

content.title: Interface Analysis
content.summary: Identifies and documents all boundaries and connections between a solution and external systems, users, hardware, and communication channels, then specifies data elements, protocols, frequency, volume, security, and error handling for each. At enterprise scale, a portfolio-level integration landscape register (one row per source-target pair, with criticality tier, sensitivity classification, and owner) precedes per-interface specification and feeds SOC2/ISO 27001/GDPR Art. 30 compliance evidence. Systems built in isolation fail at integration time; interface requirements discovered during development are the most expensive to fix.

why.p: Systems built in isolation fail at integration time. Interface requirements discovered during development are the most expensive to fix (late-change cost). At enterprise scale, undocumented "shadow integrations" — Zapier recipes, manual SFTP scripts, Excel macros — are the single most common source of audit findings and security incidents. An active-traffic reality check (90 days no traffic → retirement candidate; traffic without a register entry → governance violation) keeps the register honest.

list.item: Pre-discovery for an enterprise-wide programme (ERP migration, M&A, core-system replacement) requiring a portfolio-level interface inventory.
Drafting an integration target operating model: which team owns which interface, approval process, single source of truth.
Building a cross-system traceability matrix (capability → process → system → interface) to scope SOWs and impact analyses.
API contract governance: naming, versioning, deprecation, SLA, security baseline standards for all internal/external interfaces.
Vendor/partner onboarding with 20+ interfaces requiring a criticality-rated register with renewal dates.
Producing IT general controls evidence (SOC2, ISO 27001, GDPR Art. 30) listing every system-to-system data flow with PII classification.
Single feature with one external API call — use the per-interface spec in ba-modeling/interface-analysis.
Greenfield startup with fewer than five systems — a one-page integration diagram is enough.
Pure technical refactor (bumping protobuf version) where business capability mapping adds no decision value.
Organisation lacks a system-of-record for applications (no APM/CMDB) — build that first.

section.name: interface-types
section.p: Classify every identified connection point into one of four types before specifying it.

rule.statement: Classify every identified connection point into one of four types before specifying it; mixed-type specifications produce documents with missing fields and incomplete error handling.
rule.rationale: Each type has a distinct attribute set (protocol for system interfaces, device specifics for hardware interfaces, flow/trigger for communication interfaces); misclassification causes incomplete specifications discovered during integration testing.

list.item: User interface — human interaction points: screens, reports, forms, dashboards.
System interface — system-to-system connections: REST/SOAP APIs, file transfers, database connections, message queues.
Hardware interface — physical device connections: scanners, printers, sensors, POS terminals.
Communication interface — data exchange protocols: HTTP, SFTP, EDI X12, HL7, SWIFT, EDIFACT, fixed-width files.

section.name: five-step-analysis-procedure
section.p: Systematically identify, document, specify, and validate every interface.

rule.statement: Complete the interface questions checklist for every interface: who/what is on each end, what data is exchanged, what format, how triggered, frequency, volume, failure modes, error handling, security, and ownership.
rule.rationale: Interface specifications with missing error handling, missing volume estimates, or missing security requirements are the most common cause of integration failures discovered during performance testing or production incidents.

list.item: Step 1 — Identify: list all connection points by asking what systems connect, what data flows in/out, who are the users, what devices are involved, what external parties interact.
Step 2 — Document: capture all attributes per interface (name, type, direction, source/target, data, format, protocol, frequency, volume, security, error handling).
Step 3 — Define data elements: specify each field (element name, type, required flag, validation rules, description).
Step 4 — Specify behaviors: normal flow, error scenarios, retry logic, timeout handling, idempotency requirements.
Step 5 — Validate with stakeholders: technical teams of connected systems, users for UI interfaces, operations for monitoring, security for access control.

antipattern.description: Specifying only the happy-path flow without error scenarios.
antipattern.reason: Real integrations fail; undocumented error handling produces ad-hoc implementation decisions at development time that are inconsistent across interfaces and invisible to operations teams.

antipattern.description: Omitting volume and frequency estimates from the interface specification.
antipattern.reason: Systems cannot handle unexpected load; volume underestimates are the leading cause of production performance failures at integration points.

antipattern.description: Specifying authentication and security as "TBD" or "standard security".
antipattern.reason: Security requirements left unspecified default to the developer's judgment, producing inconsistent auth mechanisms, missing encryption, and audit failures.

antipattern.description: Interface versioning not specified at initial design time.
antipattern.reason: Unversioned interfaces break downstream consumers on every change with no migration path; versioning strategy must be decided before the interface is published.

section.name: enterprise-landscape
section.p: At scale, interface analysis becomes a portfolio governance problem. Run discovery as a four-pass process, with human sign-off at three critical gates.

rule.statement: **Pass 1 — Discovery subagent** ingests CMDB exports, API gateway access logs, iPaaS (Workato/Boomi/MuleSoft) catalogues, and produces a raw inventory. Do NOT invent interfaces not present in inputs.
rule.rationale: The discovery pass must be conservative; inventing interfaces creates phantom entries the BA must later explain and removes.

rule.statement: **Pass 2 — BA subagent** classifies each interface by business capability, criticality (1–4), data sensitivity (public/internal/confidential/restricted), and ownership; emits the integration landscape register. Capabilities must come from a closed list (the org's capability map).
rule.rationale: Capability mapping is a business judgment, not a technical metric; constraining to an approved capability list prevents hallucinated capabilities.

rule.statement: **Pass 3 — Governance subagent** diffs the register against the enterprise contract standard (naming, versioning, auth, SLA fields) and opens findings. Cite the standard line number for every finding.
rule.rationale: Structured governance rules (encoded as Spectral rules or similar) are more enforceable than written PDFs alone.

rule.statement: **Pass 4 — Human-in-the-loop review** with architecture and security before publication. Mandatory sign-offs: (1) capability-to-system mapping by Architecture, (2) sensitivity classification by Data Office, (3) tier-1 criticality by business owner, (4) deprecation decisions before retirement.
rule.rationale: These judgments involve business stakeholders and cannot be automated; mandatory sign-off creates accountability.

rule.statement: **Quarterly audit pass**: re-pull CMDB + iPaaS feeds, diff against committed register, open SDD tasks for new/retired interfaces. Any interface in the register without traffic in the gateway for 90 days → candidate for retirement; any traffic without a register entry → governance violation.
rule.rationale: Active-traffic reality checks keep the register honest and surface shadow IT.

section.name: landscape-register-schema
section.p: The portfolio-level integration landscape register is a table with stable IF-IDs that other documents can reference.

rule.statement: Each row in the landscape register represents one logical interface (source-target pair) with a stable IF-ID. Single source of truth per interface: the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Protobuf file in the owning team's repo. The register only references it (link + commit hash), never copies field-level details.
rule.rationale: Duplicating interface specs across the register and per-interface documents creates drift; one source of truth is enforceable via CI checks.

rule.statement: Capability-first IDs: tie every interface to a business capability code (`CAP-FIN-AR-01`) so retirement decisions are made at capability level, not by team.
rule.rationale: When a business capability retires (e.g., manual expense reports → automated), all its interfaces are retirement candidates; capability-first IDs make this traceability explicit.

rule.statement: Criticality tiers drive policy: tier-1 interfaces require contract tests, breaking-change review, and 24/7 monitoring; tier-4 needs only the register row.
rule.rationale: Criticality is a business judgment about impact; different tiers justify different governance overhead.

rule.statement: Data sensitivity inherited from data catalogue: do not re-classify at interface level; pull from Purview/Collibra/Atlan. If unknown, emit `unknown` and surface for human review.
rule.rationale: Field-level truth lives in the data catalogue; interface-level classification is a lossy aggregate.

rule.statement: Deprecation calendar: every interface has a `valid-until` date or "evergreen"; agents scan quarterly and warn on stale ones.
rule.rationale: Interfaces without planned retirement are governance debt; date-stamping them surfaces the decision for renewal or retirement.

section.name: governance-and-standards
section.p: Encode interface standards as code (Spectral rules) rather than written PDFs; humans approve exceptions only.

rule.statement: Standards as code: encode the API contract standard as Spectral rules and run them in every team's CI; written PDFs alone are ignored.
rule.rationale: CI gates enforce compliance on every change; governance boards review exceptions only. This scales to polyglot portfolios.

rule.statement: Two views, one register: capability view for executives (heatmap), technical view for engineers (sequence + auth); generate both from the same register.
rule.rationale: Single register, multiple views prevents stakeholder groups from seeing inconsistent state.

rule.statement: Federated governance over central: a small standards team curates rules; product teams own their interfaces; quarterly review board handles exceptions only.
rule.rationale: Centralized governance becomes a bottleneck; federated with clear escalation paths scales to large portfolios.

rule.statement: Plain-text artefacts: register in Markdown/CSV in Git, not in a proprietary EA tool only — agents need to read and diff.
rule.rationale: Plain text in Git enables CI checks, diff reviews, and automated governance; proprietary tools are agent-unfriendly.

antipattern.description: Inventory rot: an interface register collected by spreadsheet ages within one quarter; without an automated feed (CMDB + API gateway logs + iPaaS catalogue) the doc becomes a liability.
antipattern.reason: Automate the discovery pass quarterly; diff against the committed register to surface drift. Make the audit part of the SDD cycle.

antipattern.description: Shadow integrations (Zapier, Make, manual SFTP scripts on someone's laptop, Excel macros pulling from SharePoint) never make it onto the BA's diagram and cause every audit finding.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly task a separate discovery pass against email/Slack/Workato/Power Automate logs to surface candidates. Make shadow IT discovery a sub-task.

antipattern.description: Capability-to-system mapping disagrees across Architecture, Finance, and IT — the BA inherits the political fight, not just the modelling task.
antipattern.reason: Get architecture sign-off on the capability-to-system map before launching the landscape discovery. Store the mapping as a separate approved artifact.

antipattern.description: Governance theatre: contract review boards approve in hours what teams need to ship in minutes; ungoverned channels appear (slack DMs, untracked dev keys).
antipattern.reason: Encode governance rules in CI (Spectral, oasdiff) so teams get instant feedback; escalate only genuine exceptions to a review board.

antipattern.description: PII/data-classification is best-guessed at landscape level; field-level truth lives in the per-interface spec, so the landscape view is structurally lossy.
antipattern.reason: Pull sensitivity classification from the data catalogue (Purview/Collibra/Atlan), not from interface specs. Mark unknowns explicitly.

antipattern.description: LLM agents over-collapse heterogeneous flows ("all integrations are REST") — async/file/EDI/SWIFT/HL7 v2/EDIFACT channels are routinely misclassified.
antipattern.reason: Require the agent to cite a payload sample or gateway log line per row. Preserve the protocol family in a separate column for diversity.

antipattern.description: Agents conflate "interface" with "endpoint" — one interface can have many endpoints; force the prompt to use IF-ID = logical contract, not URL path.
antipattern.reason: Define interface explicitly in the prompt: "one logical contract between source and target; one row per source-target pair; multiple endpoints under one interface are subsumed."

antipattern.description: Direction perspective drift: across a portfolio, "inbound" depends on which system you sit at. Anchor each row to source→target, never relative terms.
antipattern.reason: Enforce source→target direction in every row. Use consistent naming (Source, Target, not Upstream/Downstream).

antipattern.description: Channel misclassification (REST vs RPC vs Webhook vs Event) — require the agent to cite a payload sample or gateway log line per row.
antipattern.reason: Require evidence: each row has a `payload_sample_link` or `gateway_log_ref`. Reject rows without evidence.

antipattern.description: Capability mapping invented from system names: agents hallucinate capability codes; require a closed list (the org's capability map) as input.
antipattern.reason: Pass the capability map to the agent as a closed vocabulary. Reject any capability code not in the list.

antipattern.description: Sensitivity classification: agents default to "internal" when unsure — instead instruct them to emit `unknown` and surface for human review.
antipattern.reason: Forbid the agent from guessing; mandate `unknown` for any row without explicit classification in the source system.

antipattern.description: Owner fields auto-filled from system name pattern guesses; only accept owners present in CMDB output.
antipattern.reason: Validate every owner against the CMDB extract. Reject agents' guesses; flag as `tbd` and escalate to human.

antipattern.description: Criticality is a business judgement, not a technical metric — never let the agent set tier-1 without explicit human sign-off.
antipattern.reason: Forbid the agent from setting criticality > 2 without explicit approval. Tier-1 requires business owner sign-off.

antipattern.description: Long-tail integrations (EDI X12, SWIFT, HL7 v2, EDIFACT, fixed-width files) get squashed into "file transfer" — preserve the protocol family in a separate column.
antipattern.reason: Keep protocol, channel, and format as separate columns. Preserve heterogeneity.

antipattern.description: Shadow IT: agents will not find what is not in their inputs. Explicitly task a separate pass against email/Slack/Workato/Power Automate to surface candidates.
antipattern.reason: Shadow IT discovery is a separate pass with human spot-checking. Do not expect agents to find what is hidden.

list.item: Single source of truth per interface: the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Protobuf file in the owning team's repo. The register only references it.
Capability-first IDs: tie every interface to a business capability code so retirement decisions are made at capability level.
Criticality tiers drive policy: tier-1 requires contract tests and 24/7 monitoring; tier-4 needs only the register row.
Standards as code: encode the API contract standard as Spectral rules and run them in every team's CI.
Deprecation calendar: every interface has a `valid-until` date or "evergreen".
Data sensitivity inherited from data catalogue: pull from Purview/Collibra/Atlan, not from interface specs.
Two views, one register: capability view for executives (heatmap), technical view for engineers (sequence + auth).
Active-traffic reality check: 90 days no traffic → retirement candidate; traffic without a register entry → governance violation.
Federated governance over central: small standards team curates rules; product teams own interfaces; board handles exceptions only.
Plain-text artefacts: register in Markdown/CSV in Git for CI checks and automated governance.

template.purpose: Per-interface spec: overview, connected systems, data spec, technical spec, operational spec, error handling, security.
Interface catalog summary table with diagram placeholder and links to per-interface specs.
Portfolio landscape register table (IF-ID, Source, Target, Channel, Criticality, Sensitivity, Owner, SLA, Standard?).
Script: merge CMDB export + gateway dump → starter register.csv for BA enrichment.
